attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The cylinder is viewed from a slightly elevated angle looking down, making the top circular cap visible as a flat ellipse. The camera is positioned frontally with respect to the cylinder's vertical axis, using an orthographic-like projection.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The cylinder is viewed from a slightly elevated angle, with the top cap appearing as a white ellipse. The camera has an orthographic projection and is centered on the pole.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are nearly identical, with the top ellipse and overall perspective matching very closely.
discrepancies: The pole is static; it does not animate/rotate to demonstrate the illusion of vertical motion.
No user interface controls are present to interactively rotate the pole or adjust parameters.
evaluation.notes: Excellent visual faithfulness and styling, but lacks any movement or controls to demonstrate the barber-pole illusion.
action_items: Add an animation loop to rotate the poleGroup around the Y-axis so the illusion of downward motion is actively visible.
Introduce UI controls including a Play/Pause button and a rotation speed slider.

Add a toggle to switch between 'Orthographic Front View' (which produces the illusion) and '3D perspective perspective view' (which allows the user to rotate the camera and see that the actual motion is rotation, thereby resolving the aperture problem).

feedback.rationale: While the visual geometry and faithfulness are excellent, the figure lacks the core interactive animation and controls needed to demonstrate the barber-pole illusion.
action_items: Add an animation loop to rotate the poleGroup around the Y-axis so the illusion of downward motion is actively visible.
Introduce UI controls including a Play/Pause button and a rotation speed slider.

Add a toggle to switch between 'Orthographic Front View' (which produces the illusion) and '3D perspective perspective view' (which allows the user to rotate the camera and see that the actual motion is rotation, thereby resolving the aperture problem).

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a vertical cylinder (barber pole) from a slightly elevated viewpoint, looking slightly down onto its top elliptical cap. A curved black arrow representing rotational motion is positioned directly above the cylinder, and a straight vertical arrow pointing downwards is on the right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot matches the source viewpoint closely, depicting the vertical cylinder with a slightly elevated camera showing the white top cap, with the curved arrow above and the vertical arrow on the right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are nearly identical. The elevation, centering, and perspective match the original 2D diagram perfectly, making it an excellent drop-in replacement.
discrepancies: The triangular arrowhead on the curved rotation arrow is slightly chunkier than the sleek hand-drawn arrow in the original.
The observed motion arrow is positioned slightly lower relative to the 'Observed motion' text compared to the original layout.
evaluation.notes: An exceptionally faithful, beautiful, and pedagogically rich 3D reconstruction of the barber pole illusion with excellent interactive controls.
action_items: Refine the arrowhead shape on the curved rotation arrow to be slightly sleeker and align more closely with the source's elegant curved tip.
Slightly shift the observed downward arrow upwards to reduce the vertical gap between the arrow and the 'Observed motion' text.
Consider adding a subtle toggle to switch between a full cylinder view and an apertured/masked view to further enhance the explanation of the aperture problem.

feedback.rationale: The rendered figure achieved perfect scores across all categories, has no failure modes, and matches the original perfectly; further refinement of minor visual details is unnecessary.
action_items: Refine the arrowhead shape on the curved rotation arrow to be slightly sleeker and align more closely with the source's elegant curved tip.

Consider adding a subtle toggle to switch between a full cylinder view and an apertured/masked view to further enhance the explanation of the aperture problem.
